title: Hot-process herbal soap
subtitle: An oven-cooked cold-process bar, ready to use within a week
excerpt: A hot-process bar soap cooked in the oven after blending to trace. The heat completes saponification in 1-2 hours instead of 4-6 weeks, so the bar is usable within a week. Essential oils and herbs added after cooking retain more of their fragrance than in cold-process.
sourceNotes: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), Ch. 6, hot-process soap in a crock pot and oven. Kevin Dunn, Caveman Chemistry (2003), Ch. 9, the temperature effect on saponification rate. SoapCalc SAP-value tables (v2024): olive 0.135, coconut 0.190, castor 0.128.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles to 2 kg; use a larger oven-safe pot.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Active work 2-3 hours. Bars usable in 1 week, much better at 4 weeks.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years in a cool dark cupboard. Hot-process bars dry out slightly faster than cold-process, so the bars harden well but can crumble at the edges if stored without wrapping.

glossaryTerms.definition: A soap-making method where the batter is cooked after blending to trace, speeding saponification to completion in 1-2 hours. The resulting soap is fully saponified immediately and can be used in 1-2 weeks rather than 4-6.
The percentage of oil left unsaponified. At 5% superfat this bar has extra conditioning oil.
The point where batter thickens to warm-custard consistency, the starting point for the cook phase in hot-process.

The exothermic stage when soap batter turns translucent and gel-like as saponification proceeds. In hot-process, the oven holds the batter at the gel-phase temperature until saponification is complete.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: The soap is firm and saponification is complete. Unmould and cut into 130 g bars. The surface may be more rustic than cold-process; hot-process bars have a more textured, hand-worked finish. Stand on a wire rack for 1 week to firm further.

projectSchedule.title: Bars are ready to use
projectSchedule.body: One week in. The bars are hard, fully saponified, and ready to use. Unlike cold-process, the cure is a hardening-only process rather than a saponification-completion process. The bars will improve further over 4 weeks.

content.text: A
hot-process

herbal soap. The oil and lye are blended to trace exactly as in cold-process, then the batter goes into a warm oven at 80°C to complete saponification in 1-2 hours rather than 4-6 weeks. The oven holds the batter at the temperature of the
gel phase
until the soap is finished. Essential oils and dried herbs are stirred into the finished soap mass rather than into raw batter, which preserves more fragrance.
The lye calculation is the same as a standard cold-process bar: (600 × 0.135) + (300 × 0.190) + (100 × 0.128) = 150.8 g NaOH at 0%
superfat
. At 5%: 143 g NaOH. Water at 33%: 330 g distilled water.
What you need

items.prepNote: refined olive oil. 60% of the oil weight.
76°F-melt-point coconut oil. 30% of the oil weight.
cold-pressed castor oil. 10% of the oil weight.
distilled or deionised. 33% of the oil weight.
99% pure cosmetic-grade NaOH. 5% superfat. 0.1 g precision.
rose geranium or lavender essential oil. Added to the cooked soap mass after the oven phase, when the temperature has dropped below 65°C. This preserves more fragrance than cold-process addition.

content.text: Method
Preheat the oven and kit out

Preheat the oven to 80°C (fan or conventional). Put on splash-rated goggles and 8-mil nitrile gloves. Have a large oven-safe pot with a tight-fitting lid ready; a heavy stainless-steel saucepan or an enamelled casserole works. Open a window.
Mix the lye and melt the oils

Add 143 g NaOH to 330 g distilled water in the HDPE pitcher, stirring. Set aside to cool to 38-42°C. Melt the coconut oil with the olive and castor oils in the oven-safe pot over a low heat to 38-42°C.
Blend to trace, then into the oven
Pour the lye into the oils in the oven-safe pot. Pulse the stick blender in 10-second bursts until the batter reaches
light trace

. Put the lid on the pot and place in the preheated oven. Cook at 80°C for 60-90 minutes. After 30 minutes the batter will enter a visible gel phase, turning translucent and gel-like from the centre outward. Check every 30 minutes; stir if the edges are cooking faster than the centre.
Test for doneness, add fragrance, mould

Hot-process soap is done when the batter looks like mashed potato: dry, glossy, and pulling away from the pot sides. The zap test: touch a tiny amount to the tip of your tongue; if it zaps (a sharp, brief electrical-sting sensation), the lye is not yet fully saponified and the soap needs another 20 minutes in the oven. No zap means the soap is done.

Remove the pot from the oven. Allow the soap mass to cool to below 65°C. Add the 20 ml of rose geranium essential oil and fold through with the silicone spatula. The soap will be thick and lumpy; press and fold rather than stir. Transfer the soap mass into the silicone loaf mould, pressing firmly to fill all gaps and smooth the surface with the wet back of a spoon. Leave uncovered for 24 hours.
Cure: 1-4 weeks

Unmould after 24 hours. The bars will have a more rustic, textured surface than cold-process. Cut into bars; use within 1 week of unmoulding. The bars improve significantly over 4 weeks as residual moisture evaporates and the bar hardens.
Patch test
Patch-test on the inside forearm for 24-48 hours before regular use.
Storage and shelf life

Two years in a cool dark cupboard. Wrap individually in greaseproof paper to prevent the bars from drying at the edges; hot-process bars are harder than cold-process from the start and can become brittle if left completely unwrapped in a dry room.
